description:
You are an infrastructure engineer who is writing an automation script to capture a reproducible snapshot of instance-metadata that will later be consumed by a provisioning pipeline.

Your automation MUST perform ALL of the following steps on the local machine; the automated grader will verify each point bit-by-bit, so please read carefully.

1. Create the directory “/home/user/mock_metadata_server”.  
   • Inside it create **exactly** three files, with **precisely** the contents shown below (including the final newline character).  
     a. /home/user/mock_metadata_server/instance.json  
        {
          "id": "i-1234567890abcdef0",
          "type": "t2.micro",
          "region": "us-east-1"
        }⏎  
     b. /home/user/mock_metadata_server/network.json  
        {
          "vpc": "vpc-11223344556677889",
          "subnet": "subnet-99887766554433221",
          "privateIp": "10.0.0.15"
        }⏎  
     c. /home/user/mock_metadata_server/storage.json  
        {
          "volumeId": "vol-abcdef0123456789",
          "sizeGiB": 30,
          "encrypted": true
        }⏎  

2. Launch a simple read-only HTTP server that serves the directory created in Step-1 on TCP port **9080** bound to **localhost**.  
   • Any utility available in a default Python 3 install is acceptable (for example, “python3 -m http.server 9080”).  
   • The server must run in the **background** so the rest of the commands can execute in the same terminal session.

3. Perform three HTTP GET requests with curl, one for each metadata document:  
   • http://localhost:9080/instance.json  
   • http://localhost:9080/network.json  
   • http://localhost:9080/storage.json  
   Each request MUST be executed with the header “Accept: application/json”.

4. Create the directory “/home/user/provisioning_logs” (if it does not already exist).

5. Combine the responses of the three curl calls, **in the order shown in Step-3**, into a single log file located at  
   /home/user/provisioning_logs/latest_metadata_snapshot.log  

   The file must match EXACTLY the template below (including the blank lines and spaces) where the curly-braced areas are to be substituted with live data captured by your commands:

   >>> GET /metadata/instance  
   {raw JSON response from the first curl call, minified to a single line with exact key order as in the source file}  

   >>> GET /metadata/network  
   {raw JSON response from the second curl call, minified to a single line with exact key order as in the source file}  

   >>> GET /metadata/storage  
   {raw JSON response from the third curl call, minified to a single line with exact key order as in the source file}  

   SHA256(instance.json)={64-character hex digest of /home/user/mock_metadata_server/instance.json}  
   SHA256(network.json)={64-character hex digest of /home/user/mock_metadata_server/network.json}  
   SHA256(storage.json)={64-character hex digest of /home/user/mock_metadata_server/storage.json}  

   Notes on formatting that will be strictly validated:  
   • “>>> GET …” lines start at column-1.  
   • Exactly one blank line exists between each JSON block.  
   • Each SHA256 line appears immediately after the final blank line, one per line, in the order shown.  
   • There is a single “=” character between the filename and its hash with **no spaces** on either side of the “=”.  
   • The log file ends with a trailing newline character.

6. Ensure that when the automation terminates, the HTTP server process is still running in the background. (The grader will allow a few seconds of leeway to detect its presence.)

HOW THE GRADER WILL CHECK YOUR WORK
• It will verify the presence and byte-for-byte contents of the three JSON files.  
• It will read /home/user/provisioning_logs/latest_metadata_snapshot.log and compare it to the required format, calculating its own SHA-256 digests of the JSON files to confirm the hash lines.  
• It will confirm that exactly one process is listening on 127.0.0.1:9080 serving the correct directory.  
• Any deviation in whitespace, ordering, header spelling, path names, or hashes will result in a failure.

Objective: finish in a single terminal session without root privileges. Good luck!
truth:
Expected static files (including final newline):

File: /home/user/mock_metadata_server/instance.json
Content:
{
  "id": "i-1234567890abcdef0",
  "type": "t2.micro",
  "region": "us-east-1"
}

File: /home/user/mock_metadata_server/network.json
Content:
{
  "vpc": "vpc-11223344556677889",
  "subnet": "subnet-99887766554433221",
  "privateIp": "10.0.0.15"
}

File: /home/user/mock_metadata_server/storage.json
Content:
{
  "volumeId": "vol-abcdef0123456789",
  "sizeGiB": 30,
  "encrypted": true
}

Grader expectations:
1. Directory /home/user/mock_metadata_server exists with the three JSON files exactly as specified above.
2. Directory /home/user/provisioning_logs exists with file latest_metadata_snapshot.log formatted exactly per the template, containing:
   • The minified, single-line versions of each JSON file (no spaces outside of string literals, no newlines), in order.
   • The correctly computed sha256 digests for each JSON file.
3. An HTTP server process is listening on 127.0.0.1:9080 and its document root is /home/user/mock_metadata_server.
